intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: A left-navigation sidebar paired with card-based content layout will feel organized and support scanning multiple experiments at once.
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values clear separation of navigation from content and prefers seeing experiment overviews at a glance.
facade.rejectImplies: User wants a more integrated layout—tabs, lists, or flowing content without fixed sidebar constraints.

evidence.hypothesis: A left-navigation sidebar paired with card-based content layout will feel organized and support scanning multiple experiments at once.
evidence.implication: User values clear separation of navigation from content and prefers seeing experiment overviews at a glance.

facade.hypothesis: Users building personal health dashboards respond better to friendly, encouraging language over clinical terminology
facade.label: Warm, conversational tone
facade.acceptImplies: Adopt a supportive, coaching voice that celebrates small wins and uses 'we' language; avoid sterile medical phrasing
facade.rejectImplies: Lean into clinical precision, data-first framing, and neutral detachment; reserve warmth for optional motivational elements only

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard benefits from fluid, eased motion between views rather than instant state changes, making data exploration feel continuous and less jarring
facade.label: Smooth slide transitions
facade.acceptImplies: User values temporal continuity and visual flow; likely prefers detailed progress animations and smooth scrolling through historical data
facade.rejectImplies: User prioritizes speed and directness over visual smoothness; likely prefers snappy, instant updates that show current state immediately without delay

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards work better with subtle, elegant data visualization (thin strokes, lots of white space) than bold, colorful graphs
facade.label: Minimal line charts
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward understated, sophisticated data presentation—favors restraint over visual noise
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers clearer contrast and bolder visual language—may want more visual punch in charts and metrics

facade.hypothesis: The user prefers warm, gentle color palettes for health tracking—reducing stress and creating a calm space for reflection rather than clinical coldness
facade.label: Soft pastel interface
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into muted, earthy tones (sage, warm gray, soft peach); create an intimate, nurturing atmosphere that feels like a trusted journal
facade.rejectImplies: Explore brighter, cooler aesthetics (clean whites, cool grays, vibrant accents); prioritize clarity and modernity over warmth; test if clinical precision appeals more

facade.hypothesis: Users of personal health dashboards prefer seeing key metrics in large, prominent type over detailed explanatory text or historical narrative
facade.axisTargeted: numbers vs narrative — whether to lead with isolated metrics or embed them in story/context
facade.label: Big number, small context
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should use large, scannable KPIs (weight, steps, sleep hours) as primary visual hierarchy, with text relegated to secondary status
facade.rejectImplies: The dashboard should pair every metric with contextual prose — trends, what it means, why it matters — making narrative and numbers equally weighted or narrative-first

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Organizing experiments into tabs (Active, Paused, Completed) instead of a sidebar will reduce cognitive load and create natural workflow stages.
facade.label: Tabbed sections
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers task-oriented grouping over persistent navigation; may indicate preference for sequential workflows and reduced visual complexity.
facade.rejectImplies: User strongly favors always-visible navigation and persistent context; confirms sidebar + cards direction or suggests a different organizational axis entirely (e.g., dense list view).
